organizational_information.how_long_has_the_organization_been_in_ex: Founded in 2011; in continuous operation for 14 years as of 2025
organizational_information.percentage_of_the_people_you_serve_who_u: Approximately 72% of the young people and families we serve engage with our programs multiple times over the course of a year. For example, many students start with our after-school homework help, then return for SAT/ACT prep, financial aid workshops, and summer STEM camps. A typical pattern is a student joining in 9th grade for tutoring, coming back 2–3 times per week throughout the school year, and then enrolling in at least one multi-week summer program. Families often attend several workshops per year (college readiness, mental health awareness, and parent advocacy training). We also see repeat participation across school years: about 55% of students enrolled in one academic year return the following year. This high repeat-usage rate reflects both the multi-year nature of our services and the trust we have built with students and caregivers, particularly those from low-income and first-generation college-going households.
organizational_information.other_non_profit_agencies_that_your_orga:
We maintain active partnerships with several local and regional non-profit agencies to coordinate services and reduce duplication:

- Nevada Youth Coalition – We co-host quarterly youth leadership summits and share referral pathways for students who need more intensive mentoring or mental health support.
- Southern Nevada Food Alliance – Provides weekly snack and meal support for our after-school programs; we, in turn, refer families to their mobile pantry events.
- Las Vegas Literacy Collaborative – Collaborates on reading intervention curricula for middle school students and shares volunteer reading tutors during peak times.
- Desert Springs Community Health Clinic (non-profit FQHC) – Offers on-site health education sessions twice per semester at our center and fast-track appointment scheduling for our students needing physicals or counseling.
- Clark County Public Education Foundation – Partners with us on college and career readiness events and shares data (in aggregate form) on scholarship applications and awards for our participants.
- East Las Vegas Community Center (City of Las Vegas) – While city-run, we treat them as a core community partner, co-locating some of our weekend workshops and using their gym space for youth leadership retreats.

These partnerships are formalized through MOUs that outline referral protocols, data-sharing agreements (in compliance with FERPA where applicable), and joint fundraising or event-planning expectations.
organizational_information.how_often_does_your_board_meet: The Board of Directors meets bi-monthly (six times per year), typically on the second Thursday of every other month. In addition, we hold one full-day annual retreat for strategic planning and conduct special meetings as needed for urgent decisions (e.g., real estate, executive transitions).
organizational_information.members_of_the_board_of_directors:
Current Board of Directors (as of 2025-12-11):

1. Maria L. Hernandez – Board Chair; Senior Vice President of Community Lending, Desert Horizon Bank
2. Anthony J. Collins – Vice Chair; Principal, Sunrise High School (Clark County School District)
3. Denise K. Marshall – Treasurer; CPA and Partner, Marshall & Singh Accounting Group
4. Robert "Bob" A. Kim – Secretary; Attorney, Kim & Patel Law Group, specializing in non-profit and education law
5. Jasmine R. Ortega – Director; Program Manager, Nevada Community Health Alliance
6. Leonard P. Brooks – Director; Retired Air Force Officer and current corporate training consultant
7. Priya Desai, Ph.D. – Director; Associate Professor of Educational Psychology, University of Nevada, Las Vegas
8. Cynthia "Cindy" Morales – Director; Owner, Morales Family Market and long-time neighborhood resident
9. Thomas W. Gallagher – Director; Regional Operations Director, SilverLine Technology Solutions

All board members serve staggered three-year terms, with a two-term limit, and represent a mix of education, finance, legal, health, corporate, and community perspectives. At present, 6 of the 9 board members are people of color, and 5 of the 9 grew up in the same East Las Vegas neighborhoods we serve.
organizational_information.names_and_titles_of_the_leadership_of_yo:
Key leadership of Silver Desert Youth Success Center:

- Elena Martinez – Executive Director
  Elena is the founding Executive Director and has led the organization since 2011. She previously worked as a high school counselor in the Clark County School District and holds a Master’s in Education (Counseling) from UNLV. She oversees overall strategy, fundraising, and external partnerships.

- Michael Chen – Director of Programs
  Michael joined the organization in 2016 after serving as a Teach For America corps member and later a curriculum specialist. He manages all youth-facing programs, including after-school tutoring, college and career readiness initiatives, and summer enrichment. He supervises program coordinators and ensures alignment with evidence-based practices.

- Sandra "Sandy" Lopez – Director of Operations and Finance
  Sandy is responsible for budgeting, financial reporting, HR, and facilities. She has 12 years of non-profit finance experience and is a Certified Nonprofit Accounting Professional (CNAP). She works closely with the Treasurer and Finance Committee of the Board.

- Dr. Karen O’Neal – Director of Evaluation and Impact
  Karen joined in 2019 and leads data collection, program evaluation, and continuous improvement initiatives. She has a Ph.D. in Educational Research and previously consulted for several regional education non-profits.

- Luis Ramirez – Community Engagement and Volunteer Manager
  Luis oversees volunteer recruitment, training, and retention, as well as parent engagement efforts. He coordinates our 160+ active volunteers, many of whom are college students or retired educators.

- Aisha Thompson – Development and Communications Manager
  Aisha manages individual giving campaigns, grant writing, social media, and public relations. She works closely with the Executive Director on major donor cultivation and with program staff to document impact stories.
